" Identifying and understanding the primary and secondary trends is important because it can help make better decisions about when to enter or exit a position. For example, if the primary trend is bullish (upward) but the secondary trend experiences a brief pullback (downward correction), traders can take advantage of the opportunity to purchase the asset at a lower price in anticipation that the primary trend will continue to move upward in the long term. On the other hand, if the primary trend is bearish (downward) but there is a brief upward correction in the secondary trend, the trader can use this opportunity to sell the asset at a higher price, as the primary trend is expected to continue to move downward in the long term"

The underlying logic of the DIF (Difference) is that the short-term Exponential Moving Average (EMA) reflects the current price trend, while the long-term EMA reflects earlier price trends. Therefore, if there is a significant gap between these two EMAs, it indicates that the market is trending either upwards or downwards. The MACD Histogram oscillating near the zero line indicates the strength of the trend
